turns.speaker: host
turns.text: Developing a new drug typically takes ten to fifteen years and costs around 2.6 billion dollars, with a ninety percent failure rate. How is artificial intelligence stepping in to change this daunting process?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: AI is fundamentally accelerating the pipeline by improving our accuracy at every stage, starting with early techniques like molecular docking. While classical docking programs have been used for decades, deep learning-based scoring functions are now giving us much better predictions of how well a molecule actually binds to a target protein.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: I know docking is used in virtual screening, but how do researchers even begin to sift through libraries that contain billions of compounds?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: Instead of explicitly docking every single molecule, we now use graph neural networks and transformer-based encoders to learn the underlying patterns in these vast chemical spaces. This allows AI to efficiently navigate ultra-large libraries, like the Enamine REAL Space with its thirty-seven billion compounds, to find the most promising candidates.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: Finding a molecule that binds is one thing, but doesn't it usually fail later because of safety or how the body processes it?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: Exactly, predicting ADMET properties—absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion, and toxicity—has always been a critical bottleneck. Today, machine learning models can rapidly evaluate these properties using molecular graph representations, acting as smart filters to prioritize safe candidates before we ever enter the lab.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: Instead of just filtering existing molecules, I've heard AI can actually dream up entirely new drugs from scratch.

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: That's the exciting frontier of generative chemistry, where models like variational autoencoders and diffusion networks learn to design novel structures. They can simultaneously optimize for multiple objectives, like maximizing binding affinity while keeping the molecule soluble, non-toxic, and easy to synthesize.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: With tools like AlphaFold predicting protein structures and companies like Insilico Medicine advancing AI-designed drugs into clinical trials, are we finally seeing a real-world payoff?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: Absolutely, AlphaFold has massively expanded the number of targets available for structure-based design by predicting accurate 3D protein structures from amino acid sequences alone. Combined with generative platforms, this is shrinking drug discovery timelines from years to months, turning what was once aspirational into a tangible reality.
